content:
Quick question related to the expected_likelihood. I've implemented the log-likelihood function in Python based on your given example as follows:

def check(ch: str) -> bool:
    return ch.isalpha() or ch == " "

def likelihood(s: str) -> float:
    s = s.lower()
    res = 0
    for i in range(len(s) - 1):
        w = (s[i] if check(s[i]) else " ") + (s[i + 1] if check(s[i + 1]) else " ")
        res += log(following_char.get(w, 1)) + 1e-6  # math.log computes log in base e by default
    return res

I can confirm that applying the expected cipher to the encrypted text produces the original (performed a side-by-side comparison to the original Dante's Inferno Canto 1). However, the log-likelihood score I get is -2561.1392933732736, which is not far from but seems significantly different from the expected likelihood value -2561.1156814046317.

Any idea why this might be the case? I admit I don't quite understand the problem description, would appreciate your clarification :)

Hey CodinGamer! Your contribution has unfortunately not been moderated on time and will temporarily be removed from the system... But no worries! You can republish it anytime to give it another chance! :)
nice

Overall Impression: This is a solid, ambitious puzzle idea. Tackling MCMC and cryptanalysis on CodinGame is genuinely creative - most puzzles here are pure algorithmic grind, and this one brings in probability and search, which is refreshing.

What works well:                                                                                                                                                                                     
- Great topic choice. Substitution cipher breaking via statistical methods is a real-world technique with actual history behind it. It's the kind of problem that makes you feel like you learned something useful, not just solved an abstract puzzle.
- Dante's Inferno as source text - love the flavor. It gives the puzzle personality.
- The difficulty curve is honest. You need to understand bigram statistics, implement a search strategy, and handle edge cases. That's a meaty problem without being artificially convoluted.        
- Providing expected_likelihood as an optional sanity check is a thoughtful touch - it lets solvers verify their scoring function independently from their search logic, which helps debugging enormously.
- Providing code templates on GitHub for the bigram data shows you're thinking about the solver experience - nobody wants to hand-transcribe a 27x27 matrix.

A few suggestions that would make it even better:

- The relationship between MCMC and what you're actually asking could be made a bit more explicit. Even a one-liner like "you'll need to explore the space of possible alphabets using random swaps" would orient newcomers without giving away the solution.
- Clarifying the direction of the alphabet mapping more explicitly would save solvers some head-scratching on an uninteresting detail.
- The L(i,i+1) notation could use one more sentence on what it represents (conditional probability? raw frequency?).

But honestly — these are polish issues, not structural ones. The core puzzle is well-conceived, the test cases are thorough with matched test/validator pairs, and it fills a niche that CodinGame doesn't have much of. Nice work!
very good
cool
Cool
Cool

Constraints

Cases

Cover picture

Solution
* Your solution ignores the input [[expected_likelihood]]. Should the value be included as part of the input, or can it be omitted?

Thanks.

Du bon poulet

Goal section
* Please add a sentence to indicate the goal of the puzzle (to find out the cypher alphabet instead of decrypt the given message?).

* Please change "crypted" to "encrypted" (as "crypt" is not a verb; same reason for similar changes below).

* Please change "simple, replace" to "simple: replace".

* Please change "you" to "your".

* Please change "uncrypted" to "unencrypted".

* Please change "crypting alphabet" to "cypher alphabet" (side note: Google search shows more results for "cypher alphabet" than "cyphertext alphabet").

* Please change "crypt" to "encrypt".

* Please change "english" to "English".

* Please change "originaly" to "originally".

* Suggest to split the middle paragraph into three. The second paragraph may begin with "That works", and the third one may begin with "The likelihood of a text".

* Is the sentence "to not to make long words seem less likely, you may normalize them." relevant to this puzzle? If so, what is the way to normalize them? By using the given formula? If not, suggest to delete it to avoid confusion.

* Please explain what i and i + 1 refer to in the formula.

* Please explain how P(i, i + 1) is calculated. Or will they be found in the link you're going to include in the statement?

* What does P(Markov) = -3.1812 denote? I mean, what is the implication if the value is more negative or less negative? Can the value be zero or positive?

* The notations are kind of confusing. Correct me if I'm wrong: It seems to me that P() indicates probabilities where possible values are limited between 0 and 1. But P(Markov) is a negative value you term as "likelihood" - so P() here looks like something else and another notation should be used.

* Please change "usualy" to "usually".

* Suggest to change "calculus method" to "calculation method" (I'm not sure about this).

* Suggest to add some explanation or an example on how the likelihood figures are used in decryption.

* Please explain how the following are treated (e.g. ignored?) in the calculation of likelihood:
(a) case of letters
(b) non-letters e.g. spaces, digits and other characters

* Distribution percentages table: Please show fewer letters per line as the lines may not always be shown fully. For example, my window shows A to U only. It should look better if you use 4 lines instead of 3.

* "The complete list is given below". / "I will soon enough put a link to the whole stats": List to be added, and the last paragraph to be tidied up before the approval phase.

Input section
* Line 1 description: A bit confusing to me right now. I'll wait for more explanation as commented above before I comment on this description again.

* Please change "Everything else" to "Line 2".

* Please format "text" as a [[variable]].

* Line 2 description: A bit weird, as while it is the text to decypher, the requirement is not to decypher it. Again, I will comment on this description again later.

My comment is too long, so I'll continue in another one.

Output section
* Please indicate that the alphabet is shown in uppercase.

Constraints section
* Please format "text" as a [[variable]], without formatting "Length of the".

* Please add "1 <" in front of the constraint.

Cases
* In the last pair of cases, although there is a letter which "never appears", there is only one "unknown" letter in the alphabet. So, it can actually be deduced and included in the alphabet? (Of course, you may choose to still show it as a dot.)

Thanks.

I think I put it as an help, so you know when to stop, but that's not really necessary, I know I don't use it, it is on purpose, Canto 3 is missing but that's not the only one, I don't remember why but I believe it is too short to be correctly decrypted.

Well thank you for your comment, I think it's the first constructive one. I'll take it into account when I have some time (truth is, I made that more than 2 years ago, so I don't remember exactly what is the probability, I'll have to search)

Is it copied correctly? The +1e-6 should go inside the log, it's there to avoid ln(0) but it can make a difference with numbers that small.
I'd gladly clarify, what is it that you don't get?

Well I checked, with what I said above corrected, you get -2561.1156434046525,
to reach -2561.1156814046317 you need to ignore the punctuation, don't replace it by spaces, or, if you do so, consider adding following_char["  "] = 1-1e6, that way, the ln = 0 and it doesn't matter

I see, thanks for the correction! I do get the intended likelihood score now.

I am running Metropolis-Hastings MCMC using random seed value of 42, with 1000 samples and burn-in rate of 20%. Trying different seed values show that other ciphers with greater log-likelihoods than the expected cipher are also possible.

I am not too familiar with MCMC so I could be doing this wrong. But is it possible that the expected cipher does not yield the maximum possible log-likelihood score? In this case, would the expected log-likelihood score be required for convergence to the intended answer?

That is weird, I'll take a look at that when I get back home. I thought I did my cases correctly but it could be normal (in which case my case would be false). Could you send me your code if you have more question, on discord preferably at .diabetos

I'm not in the discord channel unfortunately. I can just drop it here:

from math import log
from random import random, sample, seed
from sys import stderr

# copy prob distribution here; I renamed it CD and FC

def check(ch: str) -> bool:
    return ch.isalpha() or ch == " "

def decode(cipher: str, *, to_lower: bool = True) -> str:
    plaintext = ""
    for ch in ENCRYPTED:
        if not ch.isalpha():
            plaintext += ch
        else:
            plaintext += chr(cipher.find(ch.upper()) + (65, 97)[to_lower])
    return plaintext

def likelihood(cipher: str) -> float:
    plaintext = decode(cipher)
    res = 0
    for i in range(len(plaintext) - 1):
        w = (plaintext[i] if check(plaintext[i]) else " ") + \
            (plaintext[i + 1] if check(plaintext[i + 1]) else " ")
        if w == "  ":
            continue
        res += log(FC.get(w, 1) + 1e-6)
    return res

def proposal_distribution(guess: str) -> str:
    i, j = sample(range(26), 2)
    if i > j:
        i, j = j, i
    return guess[:i] + guess[j] + guess[i + 1:j] + guess[i] + guess[j + 1:]

def mcmc_updater(curr_state: str, curr_likelihood: float) -> tuple[str, float]:
    proposal_state = proposal_distribution(curr_state)
    prop_likelihood = likelihood(proposal_state)
    accept_crit = curr_likelihood / prop_likelihood
    accept_threshold = random()
    if accept_crit > accept_threshold:
        return proposal_state, prop_likelihood
    return curr_state, curr_likelihood

def metropolis_hastings(initial_state: str, num_samples: int, burn_in: float) -> list[str]:
    samples: list[str] = []
    idx_burnin = int(burn_in * num_samples)
    curr_state = initial_state
    curr_likeli = likelihood(curr_state)
    for i in range(num_samples):
        curr_state, curr_likeli = mcmc_updater(curr_state, curr_likeli)
        if i >= idx_burnin:
            samples.append(curr_state)
    return samples

if __name__ == "__main__":
    seed(42)  # seed random values
    SAMPLES = 2000
    BURNIN = 0.2
    expect = float(input())
    ENCRYPTED = input()
    initial = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
    best = -float("inf"), initial
    for i, guess in enumerate(metropolis_hastings(initial, SAMPLES, BURNIN), start=1):
        likeli = likelihood(guess)
        best = max(best, (likeli, guess))
        if i % 50 == 0:
            print(
                f"[{i}/{int(SAMPLES * (1 - BURNIN))}]:",
                f"expect={expect},",
                f"best={best[0]},",
                f"cipher='{best[1]}'",
                file=stderr, flush=True,
            )
    seen = set(decode(best[1]))
    print("".join(ch if chr(i + 97) in seen else "." for i, ch in enumerate(best[1])))

Well, you had me frightened, I thought the issue was on my side, but it is pretty simple:
In you likelihood function, you have following_char.get(w, 1), it should be .get(w, 0). 
If the index doesn't exist it is 0% chance, not 100%. With what you have right now, impossible string such as "JXT" are considered the most plausible.
Now with that corrected it still doesn't work, I don't know if you know what to do but I'll take another look later. I quickly glimpsed your code and it doesn't seems stupid though
I see. I was under the impression that if the index didn't exist then the entire ln(L(i,i+1)+1e-6) term should be close to 0. Will take another look at this soon, thanks!

Well no, because if the index doesn't exist, it is impossible for your alphabet to be correct, so you have to be very negative. The only exception is double white space, for it happens only with punctuation, those should indeed be ln = 0

@Diabetos I managed to get a working solution by reverse-engineering yours. Not too familiar with cryptographic methods, but to me, the idea of selecting candidates based on exp(p2-p) > 0.8 seems rather arbitrary. I was also unable to obtain the desired solution for some of the test cases after only two passes; adding a third pass across the Cartesian product of the lowercase alphabet did the trick.

Is there any way to make this a little bit more friendly for players like me who aren't as familiar with statistics-based analyses? E.g., some links to reference concepts / suggested areas of exploration.

> I think I put it as an help, so you know when to stop, but that's not really necessary

Consider mentioning that somewhere in the statement.
I'll try to find a cover. Is it better now ?

Thanks.

As you said, normalizing isn't necessary (it can be used to make all likelihoods look more alike, not matter the size of the text) so I deleted that part and redone the tests with that in mind.

Difficulty level
